reasoning: The US national deposit rate for 12-month CDs currently exhibits strong downward momentum, declining for four consecutive months in early 2025 (1.82% down to 1.75%) as banks gradually lower deposit rates in anticipation of an easing monetary environment. The Fed maintained rates in May 2025, but markets expect rate cuts to resume by July. Because banks tend to lag in their repricing and have been steadily adjusting downward independent of short-term Treasury yield volatile bounces, it is highly likely that this downward deposit rate trajectory will persist through June. Consequently, the probability of an increase by June is low. Looking further out towards August and November, inflation pressures stemming from newly implemented tariffs present a tail risk. The Federal Reserve has already noted increased volatility and risks of a possible stagflationary environment. If inflation rebounds significantly by late 2025, this could halt the rate-cutting cycle or even force the Fed to raise rates, increasing the odds that deposit rates might bounce back. To reflect this mounting uncertainty over the longer term, and strictly following the guidance to increase uncertainty for distant forecasting horizons, the probability moves progressively closer to 0.5 for the later dates.
